questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT one of the standard functions of money?
options: A source of raw materials for industry
questions.explanation: The four standard functions of money are medium of exchange, store of value, unit of account, and standard of deferred payment. Providing raw materials is not a function of money.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Money serving as a 'medium of exchange' solves which problem of barter?
options: The need for a double coincidence of wants
questions.explanation: Barter requires each party to want exactly what the other offers, a double coincidence of wants. Money removes this need, since it is accepted by everyone in exchange for goods and services.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: When money is used as a 'unit of account', it provides what?
options: A way to avoid paying taxes
A physical good that can be consumed
A common measure in which prices and values are expressed
questions.explanation: As a unit of account, money gives a common yardstick for quoting prices and comparing the value of different goods, which makes calculation and record keeping possible.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: High inflation undermines which function of money most directly?
options: Its function as a store of value
Its function as a medium of exchange only
questions.explanation: When prices rise quickly, money loses purchasing power over time, so holding it becomes a poor way to store wealth. The store of value function is the one most directly weakened by inflation.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A narrow measure of the money supply (such as M1) consists mainly of what?
options: The total value of all shares traded
Currency in circulation plus current account deposits
questions.explanation: Narrow money covers the most liquid assets used directly for transactions: notes and coins in circulation plus sight (current account) deposits. Broader measures add less liquid savings and time deposits.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Commercial banks are said to 'create money' when they do what?
options: Print bank notes for the government
Make loans that become new deposits in the banking system
Collect taxes on behalf of the state
questions.explanation: When a bank grants a loan it credits the borrower's account, creating a new deposit. Since deposits are part of the money supply, lending expands the quantity of money. Only the central bank issues notes.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: If banks keep a reserve ratio of 10 percent, the simple bank deposit multiplier is:
questions.explanation: The simple deposit multiplier is the reciprocal of the reserve ratio: $1 / 0.10 = 10$. A given increase in reserves can support up to ten times as much in deposits.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: With a reserve ratio of 20 percent, an initial new deposit of 1000 could expand total deposits in the banking system by up to how much?
questions.explanation: The deposit multiplier is $1 / 0.20 = 5$, so total deposits can rise by $5 \times 1000 = 5000$ in the simple model where banks are fully loaned up and cash does not leak out.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A higher reserve requirement imposed on banks will, other things equal, do what to their ability to create credit?
questions.explanation: A higher required reserve ratio means banks must hold more of each deposit as reserves and can lend less. The deposit multiplier $1 / r$ falls, so credit creation is reduced.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Which of the following is a core function of a central bank?
options: Acting as banker to the government and to commercial banks
Setting the prices of all goods in the economy
questions.explanation: A central bank serves as banker to the government and to the commercial banks, issues notes, manages monetary policy, and acts as lender of last resort. It does not sell ordinary goods or set all prices.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The phrase 'lender of last resort' describes which central bank role?
options: Lending free money to all firms
Lending to banks facing temporary shortages of liquidity
Lending to households for house purchase
questions.explanation: As lender of last resort, the central bank stands ready to provide funds to solvent banks that face short term cash shortages, helping to prevent panics from spreading through the banking system.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: When a central bank conducts 'open market operations' by selling government bonds to the public, the money supply tends to do what?
options: Fall, as money is withdrawn from the banking system
Rise, as money flows into the banking system
questions.explanation: Selling bonds takes money out of circulation: buyers pay the central bank, draining reserves from commercial banks. With fewer reserves, banks lend less and the money supply contracts.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: If a central bank wants to increase the money supply through open market operations, it should do what?
options: Buy government bonds from the public
Reduce its own lending to banks
Sell government bonds to the public
questions.explanation: Buying bonds injects money into the economy: the central bank pays sellers, raising commercial bank reserves. Banks can then expand lending, so the money supply grows.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The quantity theory of money is usually written as the equation of exchange. Which form is correct?
questions.explanation: The equation of exchange is $MV = PT$, where $M$ is the money supply, $V$ the velocity of circulation, $P$ the price level, and $T$ the volume of transactions (often replaced by real output $Y$).
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: In the quantity theory $MV = PY$, if velocity $V$ and real output $Y$ are assumed constant, a rise in the money supply $M$ leads to what?
options: A fall in the price level $P$
A proportionate rise in the price level $P$
questions.explanation: With $V$ and $Y$ fixed, the equation forces $P$ to move in proportion to $M$. This is the monetarist conclusion that, in the long run, money growth feeds straight into inflation.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The 'velocity of circulation' of money refers to what?
options: The speed at which bank notes are printed
The interest rate paid on deposits
The average number of times a unit of money is spent on final goods in a period
The exchange rate against foreign currency
questions.explanation: Velocity is the average number of times each unit of money changes hands to buy final goods and services over a period. From $MV = PY$, it equals $PY / M$.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: In Keynes's theory of liquidity preference, the demand for money for the 'transactions motive' depends mainly on what?
options: The size of the national debt
questions.explanation: The transactions demand for money is the cash people hold to finance everyday purchases. It rises with income, since higher incomes mean more spending to be financed.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: According to liquidity preference theory, the speculative demand for money is inversely related to what?
questions.explanation: When interest rates are high, holding idle money has a high opportunity cost and bond prices are low, so people hold less money and more bonds. Thus speculative money demand falls as interest rates rise.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Other things equal, an increase in the supply of money tends to push interest rates in which direction?
options: It has no effect on interest rates
questions.explanation: With money demand given, a larger money supply means people hold more money than they wish at the current rate. They buy bonds, raising bond prices and lowering the rate of interest until demand equals supply.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes 'near money'?
options: Money that has been withdrawn from circulation
Assets that are not money but can be turned into money quickly, such as savings deposits
Foreign currency that cannot be exchanged
questions.explanation: Near money refers to highly liquid assets, such as time and savings deposits, that are not directly usable for transactions but can be converted into cash quickly and with little loss of value.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: For an asset to function well as money, a key property is that it should be what?
options: Impossible to divide into smaller units
questions.explanation: Good money must be generally acceptable, and ideally durable, portable, divisible, and limited in supply. General acceptability is essential, since money works only if people are willing to take it.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A commercial bank's balance sheet shows customer deposits as what?
options: A form of central bank reserves
questions.explanation: Deposits are money the bank owes to its customers, so they appear as liabilities. The bank's loans and its reserves are assets. Banks make profit from the gap between interest earned and interest paid.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: If the central bank raises its main policy interest rate, what is the most likely effect on borrowing and spending?
options: There is no effect at all
Borrowing and spending tend to fall
Borrowing and spending tend to rise sharply
questions.explanation: A higher policy rate raises the cost of loans and the reward for saving, so households and firms borrow and spend less. This is the standard transmission of tighter monetary policy to aggregate demand.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The cash (or liquidity) ratio that a prudent bank keeps reflects the need to balance profitability against what?
options: The ability to meet customers' withdrawals
The size of the stock market
The level of the exchange rate
questions.explanation: Lending is profitable but illiquid, while cash earns nothing but lets the bank meet withdrawals. The reserve ratio balances the desire for profit against the need to stay liquid and avoid a run.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Token money, such as modern bank notes and coins, has which characteristic?
options: Its face value equals the value of the metal it contains
It can only be used abroad
It pays interest to the holder
Its face value is greater than the value of the material it is made from
questions.explanation: Token (fiat) money is accepted because of legal status and confidence, not because the paper or metal is worth its face value. Its intrinsic material value is far below the value printed on it.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics
